records.ety: [AS. cursian, corsian, perh. of Scand. origin; cf. Dan. korse to make the sign of the cross, Sw. korsa, fr. Dan. & Sw. kors cross, Icel kross, all these Scand. words coming fr. OF. crois, croiz, fr. L. crux cross. Cf. Cross.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Cursed (k?rst) or Curst; p. pr. & vb. n. Cursing.]

defs: To call upon divine or supernatural power to send injury upon; to imprecate evil upon; to execrate.
quotes: Thou shalt not . . . curse the ruler of thy people.
Ere sunset I'll make thee curse the deed.

defs: To bring great evil upon; to be the cause of serious harm or unhappiness to; to furnish with that which will be a cause of deep trouble; to afflict or injure grievously; to harass or torment.
quotes: On impious realms and barbarous kings impose Thy plagues, and curse 'em with such sons as those.

defs: To utter imprecations or curses; to affirm or deny with imprecations; to swear.
quotes: Then began he to curse and to swear.
His spirits hear me, And yet I need must curse.

defs: An invocation of, or prayer for, harm or injury; malediction.
quotes: Lady, you know no rules of charity, Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.

defs: Evil pronounced or invoked upon another, solemnly, or in passion; subjection to, or sentence of, divine condemnation.
quotes: The priest shall write these curses in a book.
Curses, like chickens, come home to roost.

defs: The cause of great harm, evil, or misfortune; that which brings evil or severe affliction; torment.
quotes: The common curse of mankind, folly and ignorance.
All that I eat, or drink, or shall beget, Is propagated curse.
